nativeRange.habitat: The Two-Toed Amphiuma inhabits permanent and semi-permanent shallow freshwater environments across the southeastern United States, including swamps, bayous, sloughs, ditches, canals, ponds, slow-moving rivers, wet meadows, and floodplain pools. Preferred sites typically feature heavy aquatic vegetation, soft mud substrate suitable for burrowing, and dense organic debris. The species tolerates both clear and turbid water and has been recorded in moderately polluted waterways.

decorPreferences: long footprint over depth (120 cm+) to accommodate eel-like body
soft sand or fine smooth-gravel substrate for burrowing
PVC pipe shelters or smooth caves sized to the animal's diameter
large driftwood pieces and rockwork to create hiding caves
dense floating plants (duckweed, water sprite, hornwort) to diffuse light and provide cover
subdued lighting — nocturnal species, bright light causes chronic stress
tightly sealed and weighted lid (will leave water during droughts; can climb or jump out of poorly secured tanks)
moderate filtration with no exposed intakes large enough to entangle the animal
moderate water depth (animal must be able to reach the surface to breathe air)

diet.notes: The Two-Toed Amphiuma is an opportunistic aquatic carnivore. In the wild it feeds on aquatic insects, frogs, smaller salamanders, small fish, crayfish, snails, and occasionally small snakes and turtles. In captivity, large nightcrawlers (earthworms) are the most practical staple. Most individuals will also accept crayfish, raw or thawed prawns and shrimp, lean fish chunks (smelt, silverside, or tilapia fillet), and large sinking carnivore pellets, though some patience is sometimes needed when introducing non-live prey for the first time. Adults should be fed to satiation roughly once a week; juveniles benefit from more frequent feedings of around twice weekly. Always offer prey with long forceps or feeding tongs rather than by hand — the bite is powerful, can break skin, and is generally considered painful, though it is rarely medically serious. Feeder goldfish should be avoided, as they are high in fat, contain an enzyme (thiaminase) that degrades vitamin B1 over time, and carry a higher risk of introducing disease.

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
shrimp, snails, and other invertebrates (will be eaten)
conspecifics (cannibalistic toward smaller individuals; aggression between adults)
other amphibians (will predate frogs, smaller salamanders)
live aquarium plants in the substrate (uprooted by burrowing)

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke, 3000x1631. Sharp, well-lit whole-body shot on white background showing the eel-like slate-brown body, wedge-shaped head, tiny near-blind eye, and a vestigial front limb; ideal ID image.
Brian Gratwicke, 3000x1939. Crisp head close-up clearly resolving the diagnostic two-toed front limb and wedge-shaped head; tight feature crop kept as a non-recommended detail shot.

summary: The Two-Toed Amphiuma (Amphiuma means) is the longest salamander native to the United States, a fully aquatic, eel-like species reaching up to 116 cm with tiny vestigial limbs. Native to Southeast US swamps, it is a nocturnal ambush predator that delivers a painful bite and demands a large, escape-proof, species-only setup. Specialist-only.
careNotes:
The Two-Toed Amphiuma is the longest salamander species native to the United States, with confirmed wild specimens reaching 116 cm (45.7 in) total length and captive individuals documented living up to 27 years. The eel-like body has four vestigial limbs (the front pair bearing the diagnostic two toes), small functionally near-blind eyes, and a wedge-shaped head with powerful jaws. It is part of the small relict family Amphiumidae, which contains only three living species, all confined to the southeastern United States.

This is a strictly specialist species. Adult size, escape behaviour, dietary demands, longevity, and bite risk combine to make it a long-term commitment requiring a dedicated species-only setup. Some US states list amphiumas as restricted wildlife — check local regulations before acquiring.

Tank requirements: A long (minimum 120 cm footprint), tightly lidded, fully aquatic enclosure of at least 500 L is generally advised for a single adult, with larger specimens benefiting from more space. Width and length matter more than depth; the animal must be able to reach the surface to gulp air. A soft sand or smooth fine-gravel substrate is needed for burrowing — sharp gravel can abrade the skin. Provide multiple PVC pipe sections or smooth-stone caves matched to the animal's body diameter as daytime shelters. Dense floating plants help diffuse light; this species is strongly nocturnal and stressed by bright illumination. The lid must be heavy, tight, and gap-free: amphiumas are known to leave water voluntarily during droughts, can climb damp surfaces, and have been documented escaping from poorly secured enclosures.

Water quality and parameters: Excellent water quality is essential. Although tolerant of warm Southeast US conditions in the wild, captives are generally advised to be kept on the cooler side (around 18–24°C); temperatures should not exceed roughly 26–27°C. No heater is typically required in temperate indoor rooms. pH is reported to be flexible across slightly acidic to slightly basic ranges; Hardness tolerance appears broad; moderately soft to moderately hard freshwater is generally accepted. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero. This species has notably sensitive skin, so copper-based medications and many fish treatments should never be used in the same water. Any new additions to a shared setup should be quarantined separately before introduction.

Feeding: Earthworms (large nightcrawlers) are the staple of choice. Crayfish, raw shrimp, lean fish chunks, and large sinking carnivore pellets are also accepted by most individuals after a brief settling period. Feed adults to satiation once a week; overfeeding shortens lifespan and accelerates water-quality decline. Always offer prey with long forceps — bites are powerful and painful and the animal can latch on.

Behaviour and handling: Two-Toed Amphiumas are nocturnal, secretive ambush predators that spend daylight hours burrowed in substrate or coiled inside shelters. They are known to communicate with conspecifics via faint underwater clicks. Handling should be minimised and done only with thick gloves and proper restraint — bites can break skin. Despite the dramatic common names such as 'Congo Eel' and 'Lamp-Eater', the Two-Toed Amphiuma is not venomous; any injury from a bite is mechanical only.
breedingNotes: The Two-Toed Amphiuma is rarely bred in captivity. In the wild, breeding occurs via internal fertilisation — the male deposits a spermatophore (a small packet of sperm) that the female takes up — typically during winter through spring. Females lay roughly 150 to 200 eggs in moist terrestrial cavities adjacent to standing water between June and July, and coil around the clutch for the approximately five-month incubation period. Captive breeding attempts have been made but are not routine and are generally considered advanced, as standard home setups seldom provide the seasonal cues and enclosure complexity required.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Fertilisation is internal, achieved through spermatophore transfer (the male deposits a sperm packet that the female takes up). Females lay approximately 150 to 200 eggs in moist terrestrial cavities adjacent to standing water and coil around the clutch throughout the roughly five-month incubation period. Captive breeding has been attempted but is not routine; it requires a very large enclosure with both a fully aquatic zone and a moist terrestrial laying area, along with careful seasonal simulation of temperature and day-length cues.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: The Two-Toed Amphiuma reaches up to 116 cm and requires a dedicated species-only enclosure of at least 500 L with a footprint of 120 cm or more, tightly secured against escape. It is a long-lived species with documented lifespans exceeding 25 years, and it will leave water voluntarily during droughts. Its bite is powerful and painful, and it carries a meaningful risk of injuring a keeper. The species is legally restricted as wildlife in some US states. These factors together make it unsuitable for typical home aquarists.
